segments.s730-g49: بگرد is an imperative verb meaning “search/look for!” addressed to someone. The phrase follows Persian word order: verb first (imperative), then its object 'جهان' (the world). 'چار سالار من' is a genitive phrase 'my four chiefs' where من follows the noun to mark possession.
segments.s730-g50: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” هستند is the present plural copula ‘are.’ بر جان is a prepositional phrase literally ‘upon/over (my) life’ and نگهبان من is the predicate ‘my guard/protector,’ with من again marking possession.
segments.s730-g51: هریکی means ‘each one.’ زان here functions like ‘of that / of them’ (from that group). ابا is an archaic/poetic particle whose exact force is uncertain in MSS and does not carry its common modern senses here, so it is best read as a filler/colloquial particle introducing the enumeration.
segments.s730-g52: ز ایران is a prepositional phrase ‘from Iran.’ بپای اند is a poetic periphrastic present meaning ‘stand / are on the watch / are ready’ (بـ- + پای), with اند the 3rd‑person plural copula. جنگی سوار is a noun phrase ‘war‑horsemen / mounted warriors’ (adjective + noun).
segments.s730-g53: چنین هم = ‘thus/likewise’ + ‘also,’ setting up a parallel. به is the preposition ‘for/to’ and introduces the thing to be guarded; مشکوی زرین من is an attributive phrase ‘my musk‑scented, golden (one),’ with من after the adjectives marking possession (typical in poetic Persian where the head noun may be omitted and understood).
segments.s730-g54: چه here is a connective/exclamatory particle introducing the next parallel item (classical usage not exactly matching modern dictionary glosses). در خانه = ‘in the house,’ and گوهر آگین من is a compound adjectival phrase ‘my gem‑adorned / jewel‑set (house),’ with آگین qualifying گوهر and من marking possession.
segments.s730-g55: پرستار is the noun ‘caretaker/guardian.’ باشد is a jussive/subjunctive form ‘let there be’ or ‘may there be,’ used here to command or propose that such guardians exist. The numeral phrase (ده و دو هزار) supplies the quantity (classical ordering of numerals in poetry).
segments.s730-g56: همه پاک = ‘all (of them) pure/clean.’ با طوق و با گوشوار = ‘with [a] collar and with earring(s),’ where با is the preposition ‘with’ and طوق / گوشوار are ornaments; the repetition of با is a poetic doubling for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s730-g1: دو هفته برآمد is a temporal phrase in which برآمد (past) has an idiomatic sense of 'came/elapsed' — the time 'two weeks' has passed. The clause continues with an inverted order: بدو گفت شاه literally 'to him said the king', where بدو is the indirect object 'to him', گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as a past tense reporting verb), and شاه is the subject (postposed for emphasis).
segments.s730-g2: به ... و ... به ... is a series introduced by the preposition به 'to/for' (repeated before the second pair). خورشید و ماه and تخت و کلاه are paired nouns joined by و 'and'; کلاه here functions metonymically as 'crown/ceremonial headgear' though the listed sense is 'hat'. This whole phrase is what the king utters next (an object/complement of گفت).
segments.s730-g3: که introduces a subordinate content/purpose clause ('that' / 'so that'). برگویی is the classical subjunctive/optative form of گفتن with the verbal prefix بر- (here meaning 'relate/tell'); its mood expresses what is to be done (’that you tell’). آن جنگ خاقانیان is the definite object 'that war of the Khaghanians' (جنگ followed by the genitive-like modifier خاقانیان).
segments.s730-g4: ببندی is a 2nd-person singular subjunctive/imperative form (بـ- prefix + بَندی) meaning 'you bind / you gird'. کمر is the noun 'waist' (in the idiom کمر بستن = 'to gird one's loins'). همچنان 'in the same way / likewise' modifies the action, and بر میان ('on/upon the middle' — بر + میان) indicates location/position ('in the midst').
segments.s730-g5: بدو گفت is again 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'), followed by the vocative شاها 'O king!'. انوشه is a proper name (Anoushe). The final word بدی in the segment as printed has senses listed as 'evil', but in the narrative it functions in a different role (either part of a name or a verb form) — its meaning here is not the 'evil' sense from the given list.
segments.s730-g6: روان را uses را as the definite-object marker attached to روان; in context روان can be a person/agent or 'soul' but the printed senses don't clearly match the narrative role, so its precise sense here is uncertain. به دیدار 'for/to the meeting' uses به + noun, توشه 'provisions' is the thing being given, and the final بدی again appears in a non-listed verbal/name function (not the 'evil' sense provided). Grammatically, this is a causative/benefactive construction: '(give/send) provisions to (the) meeting for the one called/identified by روان.'
segments.s730-g7: بفرمای is an honorific/command verb (from فرمود) used here as 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a purposive/subordinate clause ('so that' / 'that'), and اسپ و زین are the direct objects 'horse(s) and saddle(s)'. آورند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive/present used with تا to express the commanded action 'that they bring'.
segments.s730-g8: This is a continuation of the commanded list: کمان و کمند و کمین are nouns joined by و 'and' (bow, lasso/snare, ambush). آورند again is the 3rd-person plural verb 'they bring' (here broadly 'provide/set up'). The repeated structure (noun list + آورند) is a common classical coordinal pattern.
segments.s730-g33: بدو گفت is an inverted, archaic/poetic order meaning “(he) said to him.” بدو is the prepositional pronoun (به + او) placed before the verb (گفت) as is common in classical Persian; هنگام جنگ is a temporal prepositional phrase “at the time of battle,” with هنگام governing the noun جنگ. تبرگ is an object (a weapon-name) following the time phrase.
segments.s730-g34: بدین گونه بودم is a copular clause: بدین گونه (in this manner) is a circumstantial phrase placed before the past-tense verb بودم “I was.” چو introduces a simile “like/as,” and غرنده گرگ is the noun phrase (roaring/snarling wolf) that completes the comparison. Note the poetic word-order: manner → verb → simile.
segments.s730-g35: چنین گفت شیرین is “Shirin said thus” with چنین (“thus/so”) preposed and گفت functioning as the speech verb; شیرین is the speaker’s name. که‌ای شهریار is a vocative: که‌ای (an archaic/poetic form equivalent to ‘ای’) + شهریار = “O king.” The vocative follows the reporting clause.
segments.s730-g36: بدشمن دهی آلت کار زار is a short admonitory clause. بدشمن (‘the bad/hostile enemy’) is the indirect object or beneficiary; دهی is the second-person present/subjunctive form of ‘to give’ often used in commands/advice (contextually translated as “do not give” here); آلت کار زار is a noun phrase “the wretched instrument (آلت) of action/work (کار),” with زار qualifying it as miserable/wretched. Word order is object/recipient → verb → object phrase.
segments.s730-g37: تو با جامه پاک بر تخت زر is a descriptive prepositional cluster: تو (you) marked as topic, با جامه پاک (‘with a clean/pure robe’) specifies appearance, and بر تخت زر (‘on the golden throne’) gives location. Adjectival پاک follows the noun جامه as usual.
segments.s730-g38: ورا هر زمان برتو باشد گذر is a short optative/wish-like clause. ورا (to/there) introduces the direction/target, هر زمان ‘at every time’ is temporal, برتو (‘upon/for you’) marks the person affected, and باشد is the subjunctive/optative copula here (‘may there be’). گذر (‘passage’/‘procession’/‘visiting’) is the noun predicated by باشد. The overall sense is a prayerful wish that there will always be visits/processions to him.
segments.s730-g39: بخنده به شیرین چنین گفت شاه is a speech-introduction: بخنده (‘with a laugh’/laughingly) modifies the verb; به شیرین (‘to Shirin’) marks the addressee; چنین گفت (‘thus said’) is the reporting formula; and شاه is the subject (the king). Word order places adverbial phrase before the verb for effect.
segments.s730-g40: کزین زن جز از دوستداری مخواه is an imperative/advice clause introduced by کزین (‘from this’/‘of this’). زن (‘woman’) is the referent (this woman). جز از (‘except from’) introduces the sole thing permitted: دوستداری here functions as a nominalized notion of affection/love (‘affection/loving’), and مخواه is the negative imperative ‘do not ask/demand.’ Together they mean “Ask nothing from this woman except (her) affection.”
segments.s730-g25: بدان is a prepositional/demonstrative phrase here ('to that' or 'to that one'), formed from ب- + دان and marks the indirect object; پرِ هنر is a postpositive adjectival phrase meaning 'full of skill' (پر = 'full (of)') modifying زن 'woman'. The verb phrase بفرمود (honorific past) is placed before its subject شاه (inverted order common in classical Persian): 'the king commanded/said (to her)'.
segments.s730-g26: Simple clause with verb + direction: زن آمد is past-tense 'the woman came'. به نزدیک is a compound prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک) indicating motion toward proximity, and اسپ سیاه is the noun phrase 'the black steed' (اسپ = steed, سیاه = black) as the goal.
segments.s730-g27: بن نیزه را is the definite object phrase 'the butt/base of the spear' (بن = base/butt of, نیزه = spear), with را marking it as definite/object. بر زمین برنهاد is a past transitive verb phrase 'placed on the ground' (برنهاد = placed on/laid down), where بر + زمین indicates the surface.
segments.s730-g28: ز بالا is an ablative phrase 'from above'; بزین (ب + زین) here functions as a verb 'mount (the) saddle' (classical compound), and اندرآمد is used in the narrative past to mean 'entered/mounted/ascended' (verb-of-motion). چوباد is a simile/adverbial 'like the wind' qualifying how he/she rode (swiftly).
segments.s730-g29: به باغ اندر آورد is a light-verb construction meaning 'brought into the garden' (به باغ = into the garden, اندر آورد = brought in). گاهی گرفت is a brief adjunct 'at times (گاهی) seized/took (گرفت)' — گاهی modifies the verb to indicate occurrence occasionally.
segments.s730-g30: چپ و راست is the paired directional 'left and right' (چپ = left; وراست/وراست = toward the right), here functioning adverbially. بیگانه راهی گرفت is a verb phrase 'took a strange/foreign route' (بیگانه = strange/foreign; راهی گرفت = 'took a way/path'), so together it says he/she went off by a stranger's path to left and right.
segments.s730-g31: همی هر زمان is an emphatic temporal frame 'ever/always, at every time' (همی = constantly; هر زمان = every moment). باره برگاشتی is an action clause: باره here 'occasion/charge' and برگاشتی (past second/third-person form) 'you/he lifted/removed/opened' — in context it conveys taking up a burden or launching an action repeatedly.
segments.s730-g32: وز ابر سیه is an ablative/agentive phrase 'from/through the black cloud(s)' (وز here = blast/gust/from), and نعره برداشتی is a common classical collocation meaning 'you raised/uttered a shout' (نعره = cry/shout; برداشتی = 'took up' = 'lifted/uttered'); the verb is past narrative, linked to the preceding temporal frame.
segments.s730-g41: همی‌تاخت is the imperfective/past-continuous compound (همی- + past stem) meaning 'kept charging' or 'was charging'. گرد اندرش is a prepositional phrase: گرد = 'around' and اندر + ش (اندرش) = 'in/within him' (اندر + the enclitic ش). گردیه is a past‑participle/adjectival form (گردیده) used nominally as 'the turned/assembled (group)'; the phrase says that while he kept charging, a ring/assembly formed around him.
segments.s730-g42: برآورد is a past/preterite-type verb meaning 'brought/caused/put (into effect)'; here it carries the sense 'he would bring/put (it) into play'. گاهی = 'sometimes' (adverb). برش appears as بر + ش 'on/against him' (the provided senses 'cut' do not fit here, so the sense is effectively the locative/compound). گردیه repeats the participial/nominal 'the turned/assembled (troop)'; together the clause says 'sometimes he would bring that assembled throng upon him.'
segments.s730-g43: بدو = به + او 'to him' (here 'unto him'); مانده is the perfect/passive/adjectival form meaning 'left' or 'remained' (i.e., 'was left'). بد functions adverbially/adjectivally 'ill/struck' or 'severely'; خسرو is the proper name (Khosrow). اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment'. The whole segment means 'to him Khosrow remained (i.e. Khosrow was left) much amazed.'
segments.s730-g44: بدان is here the compound/prepositional 'به + آن' = 'at that' or 'concerning that'. برز و بالا are paired nouns/adjectives meaning 'loftiness/highness' (برز = exalted/height, بالا = high/lofty). آن یال و کفت = 'that mane and that paw/palm' (یال 'mane', کفت 'palm/hand or paw'); the sequence lists the features that caused amazement. The syntax is a simple series of nouns governed by the demonstrative 'آن/بدان'.
segments.s730-g45: چنین 'thus' introduces reported speech. گفت here is the verb 'said' (the provided lexical senses were nominal, so none match the verbal use exactly). با marks the indirect object/recipient 'to' or 'with'; گردیه is the participial/collective 'the assembled' or 'the turned (group)'; شهریار is the speaker 'the king.' Word order places the adverb first, then the verb and the addressee.
segments.s730-g46: که introduces the content of what was said ('that ...'). بی‌عیبی is an adjectival phrase 'without fault' or 'faultless'. از marks the source/standard 'from' or 'with respect to'. گردش روزگار is an idiomatic genitival phrase 'the turning/changes of time' (گردش = turn/turning, روزگار = time/fate), i.e. 'the vicissitudes of time.' The clause asserts freedom from such faults.
segments.s730-g47: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. تا here functions as a subordinating particle 'so that / in order that' linking to the following verb بیبینم (ببینم) 'I may see'. که introduces the content 'whether / that'. با جام می = 'with a cup of wine' (با = with, جام = cup, می = wine); the prepositional phrase sets the circumstance under which the speaker will test or observe.
segments.s730-g48: یکی here functions adverbially 'at all / in any way' (not strictly the numeric sense). سست باشی is a subjunctive/potential construction 'you might be weak' (سست = weak; باشی is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be'). اگر introduces the conditional 'if'; سخت پی is an adverbial phrase 'if (you are) firmly/strongly in pursuit' (سخت = firmly/strongly, پی = 'in pursuit / following'). The contrast sets a test between weakness under wine and strength in pursuit.
segments.s730-g57: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). نگهدار here functions as an instruction/title 'be (their) keeper/guardian' rather than a lexical verb form; ایشان is the object/pronoun 'them', and توی follows as a characterizing address 'you (who are) among/within them' (inversion is poetic: temporal phrase → predicate → object → vocative/locative).
segments.s730-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; با رنج و تیمار is a compound prepositional phrase 'with hardship and with care/solicitude' (رنج = suffering/hardship, تیمار = care/treatment). خویشان is the noun 'kinsmen/relatives' and the final توی again marks location/association 'among' (i.e. 'who among the kinsmen, with hardship and care').
segments.s730-g59: نخواهم is a first‑person negative of خواستن 'I do not want/wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'; گویند is the classical 3rd‑person subjunctive/jussive form used for reported speech 'they say / they should say'; زیشان is the contracted prepositional pronoun 'از ایشان' meaning 'about/of them'; سخن here means 'word/speech/account'. The overall structure: 'I do not want that they should speak about them...'.
segments.s730-g60: جز = 'except'; از تو = 'about/of you' (از + 2nd‑person pronoun). اگر ... بودگر ... کهن is a compact conditional/ concessive formula: اگر نو بودگر کهن = 'if it be new or (if) old' (بودگر = archaic fused conditional 'if it were/should be'); نو = 'new', کهن = 'old/ancient'. The phrase limits permitted speech to be only about 'you', regardless of whether the account is new or old.
segments.s730-g61: شنید is simple past 'heard' (3rd person). آن سخن = 'that speech/word' (direct object). گردیه ('gardiye') is a collective noun 'the assembly / those gathered' and functions as the subject of the following predicate; شاد شد = 'became glad / rejoiced' (شاد = glad, شد = became). Note the poetic word order: object and verb precede the subject that undergoes the change of state.
segments.s730-g62: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / out of'. بیغاره is an archaic/obscure term (lemma marked as archaic in the data) and is the source/condition in this phrase; دشمن = 'the enemy'; آزاد شد = 'became free / was freed' (آزاد = free, شد = became / was). Grammatically this is a simple verbal clause: 'the enemy was freed from X'.
segments.s730-g63: همی‌رفت is a habitual/continuous past 'he would go / he kept going'. روی زمین = 'the surface/face of the earth' (روی = surface/on, زمین = earth); را is the definite object marker emphasizing 'روی زمین' as what is being traversed. بروی is a movement verb (ب + روی) 'to go over / to pass on' (3rd‑person sense), so the clause describes continuous motion across the earth's surface.
segments.s730-g64: همی is an adverb of continuity 'continually / still'. آفرین is an exclamation 'bravo / praise' (here the object of خواند as what is uttered). خواند is simple past 'he uttered/sang/called out' (used here as 'he cried out / praised'). بر فر اوی = 'to/for his far(=glory)' — بر = 'to/for', فر = the royal 'glory' or splendour, اوی = archaic possessive 'his'. The whole clause: 'and he continually cried
segments.s730-g9: This is a coordinated noun phrase: a demonstrative 'همان' (that/the same) precedes 'نیزه' (spear). The items are linked by the conjunction 'و' (and). 'خود' here functions as a reflexive/emphatic nominal element ('itself' / 'its own [part]') referring back to the spear, and 'خفتان جنگ' is a poetic nominal formation from the verb 'خفتن' (to lie/sleep) used here to mean 'those lying in/through war' (poetic: the sleeping/fallen ones of battle). Word order is list-like (Demonstrative + Noun + و + emphatic/resultant noun + و + poetic noun phrase).
segments.s730-g10: 'یکی' (one) numerally modifies the following noun; 'ترکش' (quiver / arrow‑shaft) is written here split into 'ترک' + enclitic 'ش' — in the compound the 'ش' functions as part of the noun form (here treated as the possessive/enclitic-letter in the printed segmentation). 'آگنده' is a postpositive adjective meaning 'filled/laden' and therefore follows the noun it qualifies (typical in Persian poetic word order). 'تیر خدنگ' is a tight noun phrase in which 'خدنگ' (dagger; short, pointed blade) qualifies 'تیر' — giving 'dagger‑pointed arrows' or 'barbed/knife‑like arrows'. Overall: 'one quiver full of dagger‑arrows.'
segments.s730-g11: 'پرستنده‌ای' is an indefinite noun with the -ای marker ('a worshipper/devotee'); it is marked by the object marker 'را', so this phrase functions as the (specific) object of the verb. 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past verb form (here: 'commanded/ordered'); the subject 'شاه' (the king) follows the verb — inversion which is common in classical Persian style for emphasis or meter.
segments.s730-g12: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces the content of the king's command ('that ...'). 'درباغ گلشن' is a prepositional phrase ('in the flower‑garden' — 'در' + 'باغ گلشن' = in the garden of flowers'). 'بیارای گاه' is an archaic/poetic verbal phrase meaning roughly 'prepare/embellish a place/spot' (here 'گاه' functions as 'place/spot'); the verb's subject is omitted because it is supplied by the preceding instruction (i.e. the devotee is to do this).
segments.s730-g13: 'برفتند' is past plural ('they went/off'), placed at the start of the clause. 'بیدار دل' is a compound adjective 'awake‑hearted' (بیدار modifying دل), and 'بندگان' is the plural noun 'servants/subjects'; together they form 'the servants, with hearts awakened, departed.' The adjective phrase precedes the head noun in this poetic order.
segments.s730-g14: This is a prepositional/genitive grouping introduced by 'ز' (from/of), repeated for emphasis: 'ز ترک و ز رومی' = 'from the Turks and from the Romans'. 'پرستندگان' (worshippers/devotees) is the noun that these prepositional phrases modify or classify — i.e. they are worshippers of/among Turks and Romans. The repeated 'ز' is a poetic device to balance the line.
segments.s730-g15: 'ز' (زِ) here means 'of/among' (poetic) and introduces the group; 'خوبان رومی' = 'the Roman beauties/good ones' (خوبان = beloveds/good people, qualified by رومی). The number phrase 'هزار و دویست' (one thousand and two hundred = 1,200) quantifies that group; in Persian the quantifier often follows the noun it counts in poetic order.
segments.s730-g16: 'تو گفتی' is the past second‑person clause 'you said' (subject pronoun 'تو' + past verb 'گفتی'); in the printed lemma the verb's senses were given as 'conversation', but here it is the past tense 'you said' — sense index for that lemma is not provided, so it is marked accordingly. The clause that follows, 'به باغ اندرون راه نیست', is an existential/locative negation: 'به' = to/into, 'باغ اندرون' = the inner garden/inner quarters of the garden, and 'راه نیست' = 'there is no way/entrance' (literally 'way is not'). The subordinate clause lacks an explicit subject because it reports what was said.
segments.s730-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید is used metaphorically as 'sun' and is directly followed by شیرین, which here is the proper name Shirin (appositive: 'the sun Shirin'). به پیش اندرون is a movement phrase: به + پیش + اندرون = 'toward the front of/into the inner chamber' (به = toward/into, پیش = in front of/before, اندرون = inner quarters).
segments.s730-g18: خرامان is an adverb/adverbial participle meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' modifying the verb of motion; به بالای expresses direction 'up onto' (به = toward, بالای = atop/of the top). سیمین ستون ('silver column') is the destination; ستون is the noun 'column'. The whole string describes her striding up onto the top of the column.
segments.s730-g19: بشد گردیه is a poetic word order where بشد ('it became/it happened') combines with گردیه ('turned') to mean 'he turned' or 'he turned himself' (past action). تا به نزدیک شاه uses تا 'until' plus به نزدیک شاه 'to/near the king' to mark the endpoint of the turning/movement.
segments.s730-g20: زره خواست is a simple past clause 'he wanted/asked for armor' (زره = armor; خواست = wanted/asked). از ترک و رومی کلاه attaches a source/possessor phrase with از 'from' and a coordination 'ترک و رومی' (Turks and Romans), with کلاه 'hat/helmet' as the requested item (i.e. helmets from Turks and Romans).
segments.s730-g21: بیامد is the past tense 'came' and is modified by the adverbial خرامان 'stately/with a proud gait'. ز جای 'from her place' uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' plus جای 'place'. نشست is the past 'sat (down)'. Together: 'came stately from her place and sat.'
segments.s730-g22: کمر بر میان بست is an idiomatic verb phrase: کمر بست = 'girded (one's) waist'; بر میان بست indicates the belt was fastened at the middle (بر = on/upon, میان = middle, بست = bound/tied). و نیزه بدست is a coordinated clause 'and (with) spear in hand' where بدست means 'in the hand' (i.e. holding a spear).
segments.s730-g23: بشاه جهان is a dative/vocative phrase 'to the king of the world' (بشاه = to/the king, جهان = world). گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (the supplied senses list treats گفت nominally, but in context it is the verb 'he said'). دستور باش is an imperative/quotative core: دستور = 'order/command' and باش is the imperative 'be' or 'issue (be)'—together functioning like 'give the order' or 'be the command'.
segments.s730-g24: یکی here means 'someone' or 'let one (person)' (indefinite subject). چشم بگشا is an imperative compound 'open an eye' meaning 'keep watch/observe' (چشم = eye; بگشا = open!). ز بد دور باش is a short imperative admonition: ز = 'from', بد = 'evil/harm', دور باش = 'be far' or 'keep away'. Altogether: 'Let someone keep watch; be far from harm.'

sentences.s730-s25: Search the world for my four chiefs, who stand as guardians over my life.
sentences.s730-s26: For each one of them there are ten and two thousand war‑horsemen from Iran standing ready.
sentences.s730-s27: Likewise, place (or have) for my musk‑scented golden (garment) — and in my jewel‑adorned house — (the same).
sentences.s730-s28: Let there be ten and two thousand guardians — all pure, with collars and with earrings.
sentences.s730-s1: Two weeks later the king said to him, "For the sun and the moon, and for the throne and the crown..."
sentences.s730-s2: ‘That you may relate that war of the Khaghanians, and gird your loins likewise in the midst.’
sentences.s730-s3: He said to him, 'O king, (to) Anushah‑badi (give) — provide the traveller/party with provisions for the meeting.'
sentences.s730-s4: He commanded that they bring horses and saddles, and that they bring bows and lassos and prepare ambushes.
sentences.s730-s17: He said to him, “At the time of battle my axe was like this — I was like a snarling wolf.”
sentences.s730-s18: Shirin said thus, “O king, do not give the enemy the wretched instrument of action.”
sentences.s730-s19: You, in a pure robe on the golden throne; may there always be a passing (procession) to you there.
sentences.s730-s20: The king laughed and said to Shirin thus, “Ask nothing from this woman except affection.”
sentences.s730-s13: The king gave orders to that skillful woman; the woman came up to the black steed.
sentences.s730-s14: She set the butt of the spear on the ground; from above she mounted the saddle and rode in like the wind.
sentences.s730-s15: He/they brought (it) into the garden; at times seized (it). Then, taking left and right, he/she followed a strange/path of strangers.
sentences.s730-s16: Ever and always, at each moment you lifted the burden/you opened the case; from the black clouds you uttered a cry.
sentences.s730-s21: He kept charging; around him the assembled throng turned, and at times he hurled that throng upon him.
sentences.s730-s22: Khosrow was left there amazed; at that greatness and loftiness, and at that mane and paw.
sentences.s730-s23: Thus the king spoke to the assembled throng, saying that he was free from the faults of the turns of time.
sentences.s730-s24: Now let me see whether, with a cup of wine, you will be weak at all, or, if firm, pursue strongly.
sentences.s730-s29: From then on, be their guardian — you who are among them — who, with hardship and solicitude, are among the kinsmen.
sentences.s730-s30: I do not wish that they speak of them except of you, whether the tale be new or old.
sentences.s730-s31: They heard that speech; the assembly rejoiced, and the enemy was set free from (the) biġāre (an obscure/archaic term).
sentences.s730-s32: He kept traversing the face of the earth, and as he went he continually cried out 'bravo' to his glory.
sentences.s730-s5: That same spear, its haft and the sleepers of battle — a single quiver, laden with dagger‑like arrows.
sentences.s730-s6: The king commanded a devotee, ordering that he prepare a spot in the flower‑garden.
sentences.s730-s7: The servants, their hearts awakened, went forth — worshippers from the Turks and from the Romans.
sentences.s730-s8: Of the Roman beauties there are twelve hundred — you said there is no way into the inner garden.
sentences.s730-s9: When Shirin — like the sun — strode into the inner chamber, she moved up to the top of the silver column.
sentences.s730-s10: He turned until he was close to the king; he asked for armor and helmets from the Turks and the Romans.
sentences.s730-s11: She came forward stately from her place and sat down; she girded her waist in the middle and held a spear in her hand.
sentences.s730-s12: He said to the king of the world: 'Give the command — have someone keep watch; be far from evil.'
